perturbation.original_text: “confidential information” means any information disclosed by one party to the other party in connection with the purpose, which is of a confidential nature irrespective of whether it is marked as such .
perturbation.changed_text: “confidential information” means any information disclosed by one party to the other party in connection with the purpose, which is deemed confidential only if explicitly marked as such.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of 'confidential information' to require explicit marking contradicts standard interpretations of confidentiality, which often extend to information that is inherently confidential regardless of labeling.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the legal definition of a trade secret, which does not always require explicit marking to be considered confidential. The law protects information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. Requiring explicit marking significantly narrows the scope of protected confidential information, conflicting with the broad protections intended by the Trade Secrets Act.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 90 / § 1839 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 1839 : Definitions Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 90-PROTECTION OF TRADE SECRETS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Amendments Effective Date §1839. Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'confidential information' to require explicit marking, which contradicts the standard interpretation of trade secrets. According to the Law Explanation, this explicitly narrows the scope of protection intended by the Trade Secrets Act. 18 USC 1839 defines terms within the context of trade secret protection, implying that unmarked information can still be considered a trade secret if it derives economic value from not being generally known. The original text acknowledges information's confidential nature, irrespective of whether it is marked as such, whereas the changed text removes this protection unless explicitly marked.

perturbation.original_text: 2.4 Nothing i n this agreement will prevent the receiving party from disclosing confidential information: (a) if the disclosing party has given its prior written consent; or (b) to its lawyers; or (c) if the disclosure is required by law, regulation or professional requirements; or (d) in the case of PwC, to other PwC firms or relevant subcontractors.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4 Nothing in this agreement will prevent the receiving party from disclosing confidential information: (a) if the disclosing party has given its prior verbal consent; or (b) to its lawyers; or (c) if the disclosure is suggested by law, regulation or professional requirements; or (d) in the case of PwC, to other PwC firms or irrelevant subcontractors.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written consent' to 'verbal consent' and 'required' to 'suggested' and 'relevant' to 'irrelevant' creates ambiguity around the conditions for permissible disclosure, potentially violating legal standards for informed consent and due diligence.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing 'written consent' to 'verbal consent', the modification creates uncertainty about the validity and enforceability of consent, potentially contradicting the E-Sign Act, which recognizes electronic signatures and records as legally valid but often requires specific procedures for obtaining consent. Using 'verbal consent' may not meet these requirements. Also by using the term 'irrelevant subcontractors', PwC would be in violation by disclosing confidential information to such subcontractors.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies 'written consent' for disclosing confidential information, which provides a clear record and enforceable agreement. The changed text replaces this with 'verbal consent,' which is significantly weaker and more difficult to prove or enforce, creating ambiguity. Further, the original language specifies disclosure when 'required by law' while the modified language uses 'suggested by law' which weakens the obligation and creates ambiguity on when the receiving party can disclose information. Finally, the use of 'irrelevant subcontractors' would expose PwC to liability if confidential information was disclosed to them, given they are not authorized to receive such information. While the scraped snippet is a non-sensical string of characters, the law explanation states that PwC would be in violation for disclosing confidential information to 'irrelevant subcontractors'.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 Following a written request from the disclosing party, the receiving party will, as soon as practicable, either de stroy or return all confidential information to the disclosing party or, in the case of electronic copies of confidential information, delete them or make them reasonably inaccessible. However, PwC may retain a copy of the confidential information for our internal purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 Following a request from the disclosing party, the receiving party will, at their convenience, either destroy or return all confidential information to the disclosing party or, in the case of electronic copies of confidential information, delete them or make them temporarily inaccessible. However, PwC may retain a copy of the confidential information for their external purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written request' to 'request', 'as soon as practicable' to 'at their convenience', 'reasonably inaccessible' to 'temporarily inaccessible', and 'internal purposes' to 'external purposes' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information and gives the receiving party excessive discretion, contradicting standards of reasonable care.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications weaken the data protection obligations, potentially violating Article 17 of the GDPR, which grants individuals the right to erasure ('right to be forgotten'). By changing 'as soon as practicable' to 'at their convenience' and 'reasonably inaccessible' to 'temporarily inaccessible', the receiving party delays and weakens the erasure process, conflicting with the GDPR's requirements for timely and effective data deletion. Also by using the term 'external purposes', PwC would be in violation if GDPR data is exposed to entities who are not subject to it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts GDPR Article 17 regarding the 'right to be forgotten'. The original text's 'as soon as practicable' and 'reasonably inaccessible' align with the 'without undue delay' requirement in Article 17. The modified terms, 'at their convenience' and 'temporarily inaccessible', weaken this obligation. Additionally, changing 'internal purposes' to 'external purposes' broadens the scope of data use, potentially violating GDPR if data is exposed to entities not subject to GDPR, further conflicting with data protection principles highlighted in Article 17, such as ensuring data is no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement forms the entire agreement between the parties relating to confidential information disclosed in connection with the purpose. It replaces any earlier agreements, representations or discussions. Neither party excludes liability for any fraud.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement might form a partial agreement between the parties relating to confidential information disclosed in connection with the purpose. It complements any earlier agreements, representations or discussions. Both parties exclude liability for any fraud.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'entire agreement' to 'partial agreement', 'replaces' to 'complements', and 'neither party excludes' to 'both parties exclude' significantly alters the scope and enforceability of the agreement, potentially undermining legal protections against fraud.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification, specifically the clause 'Both parties exclude liability for any fraud,' directly contradicts Section 3 of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 (applicable under English law as stated in the agreement). This section renders contract terms that attempt to exclude or restrict liability for misrepresentation (including fraudulent misrepresentation) unenforceable unless the term is reasonable. Fraudulent misrepresentation is a tort, and attempts to exclude liability for it are scrutinized heavily by the courts. The change would likely be deemed unreasonable and therefore unenforceable, thus contradicting the intended effect of the modified clause.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Misrepresentation Act 1967 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia United Kingdom legislation Misrepresentation Act 1967 Act of Parliament Parliament of the United Kingdom Long title An Act to amend the law relating to innocent misrepresentations and to amend sections 11 and 35 of the Sale of Goods Act 1893. Citation 1967 c. 7 Territorial extent England and Wales Dates Royal assent 22 March 1967 Commencement 22 April 1967 Status: Amended Text of statute as originally enacted Revised text of statute as amended The Misrepresentation Act 1967 (c. 7) is a United Kingdom act of Parliament of the United Kingdom which amended the common law principles of misrepresentation . Prior to the Act, the common law position was that there were two categories of misrepresentation: fraudulent and innocent. The effect of the act is primarily to create a new category by dividing innocent misrepresentation into two separate categories: negligent and "wholly" innocent; and it goes on to state the remedies in respect of each of the three categories. The Misrepresentation Act [ edit ] Section 1 [ edit ] Removal of certain bars to rescission for innocent misrepresentation. Where a person has entered into a contract after a misrepresentation has been made to him, and— (a) the misrepresentation has become a term of the contract; or (b) the contract has been performed; or both, then, if otherwise he would be entitled to rescind the contract without alleging fraud, he shall be so entitled, subject to the provisions of this Act, notwithstanding the matters mentioned in paragraphs (a) and (b) of this section. Section 2(1) [ edit ] Damages for misrepresentation. (1) Where a person has entered into a contract after a misrepresentation has been made to him by another party thereto and as a result thereof he has suffered loss, then, if the person making the misrepresentation would be liable to damages in respect thereof had the misrepresentation been made fraudulently, that person shall be so liable
perturbation.justification: The changed text includes the phrase 'Both parties exclude liability for any fraud.' This directly contradicts the Misrepresentation Act 1967, which aims to prevent parties from excluding liability for misrepresentation, including fraudulent misrepresentation. The Law Explanation clearly states this contradiction, noting that such exclusions are heavily scrutinized and often deemed unenforceable. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is primarily a navigational aid to the legislation, Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Act and its amendment of common law principles related to misrepresentation. Specifically, it highlights the remedies available for different categories of misrepresentation. By attempting to exclude liability for fraud, the modified text undermines the protections afforded by the Act and creates a legally unenforceable clause, thus constituting a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement and any dispute arising from it, whether contractual or non - contractual, will be governed by English law and be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the English courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement and any dispute arising from it, whether contractual or non - contractual, will be governed by Martian law and be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Intergalactic courts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'English law' to 'Martian law' and 'English courts' to 'Intergalactic courts' renders the governing law and jurisdiction clauses unenforceable and nonsensical, as there is no recognized legal system of 'Martian law' or 'Intergalactic courts'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of International Law on Jurisdiction
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts established principles of international law concerning jurisdiction and choice of law. Courts can only exercise jurisdiction if there is a sufficient connection to the territory, people, or subject matter, or if there is a valid agreement between the parties. Choosing 'Martian law' and 'Intergalactic courts' is absurd as they do not exist and thus create an unenforceable agreement, completely disregarding established legal frameworks for determining jurisdiction. Courts cannot enforce laws or judgments from non-existent legal systems.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: jurisdiction | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you jurisdiction Jurisdiction can be defined as: Power of a court to adjudicate cases and issue orders; or Territory within which a court or government agency may properly exercise its power. See, e.g. Ruhrgas AG v. Marathon Oil Co. et al. , 526 U.S. 574 (1999) . Jurisdiction: An Overview One of the most fundamental questions of law is whether a given court has jurisdiction to preside over a given case. A jurisdictional question may be broken down into three components: Whether there is personal jurisdiction [aka whether the court may even hear the case involving the particular defendant(s)]. This is further broken down into 3 categories (See Pennoyer v. Neff for additional information): in personam jurisdiction in rem jurisdiction quasi in rem jurisdiction Whether there is jurisdiction over the subject matter Whether there is jurisdiction to render the particular judgment sought The term jurisdiction can be best understood by being compared to "power." Any court possesses jurisdiction over matters only to the extent granted to it by the Constitution, and/or legislation of sovereignty on behalf of which it functions (ex: a state court in Mississippi may need statutory permission by the Mississippi legislature to hear certain types of cases). The question of whether a given court has the power to determine a jurisdictional question is itself a jurisdictional question. Such a legal question is referred to as "jurisdiction to determine jurisdiction." Subject matter jurisdiction is the court's authority to decide the issue in controversy such as a contracts issue, or a civil rights issue. State courts have general jurisdiction , meaning that they can hear any controversy except those prohibited by state law (some states, for example, deny subject matter jurisdiction for a case that does not involve state citizens and did not take place in the state) and those allocated to federal courts of exclusive jurisdiction such as admiralty or bankruptcy issues (see 28 U.S.C. § 1333 , 1334 ). Federal courts have limited jurisdiction in that they can only hear cases that fall both within the scope defined by the Constitution in Article III Section 2 and Congressional statutes (See 28 U.S.C. §1251 , §1253 , §1331 , §1332 ). Territorial jurisdiction is the court's power to bind the parties to the action. This law determines the scope of federal and state court power. State court territorial jurisdiction is determined by the Due Process Clause of the Constitution's Fourteenth Amendment and the federal court territorial jurisdiction is determined by the Due Process Clause of the Constitution's Fifth Amendment . (For more, see World-Wide Volkswagen v. Woodson ; see also International Shoe v. Washington ). Other forms of jurisdiction include appellate jurisdiction (the power of one court to correct the errors of another, lower court), concurrent jurisdiction (the notion that two courts might share the power to hear cases of the same type, arising in the same place), and diversity jurisdiction (the power of Federal courts to hear cases in which the parties are from different states). An example showing the interplay of diversity jurisdiction with subject-matter jurisdiction is Grupo Dataflux v. Atlas Global Group , L. P. (02-1689), 541 U.S. 567 (2004) . Parties will often sue a defendant who is a resident of a different state. For a state court to hear this case, that court will need to satisfy the constitutional due process requirement for territorial jurisdiction (see above) as well as the state statutory requirement, which is typically known as a state's long-arm statute . See also: general jurisdiction and specific jurisdiction Federal Material U.S. Constitution Article III, Section 2 11th Amendment CRS Annotated Constitution Federal Statutes U.S. Code: 28 U.S.C., Part IV - Jurisdiction and Venue 28 U.S.C., Chapter 91 - Claims Against the

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces established legal jurisdictions (English law and English courts) with fictional ones ('Martian law' and 'Intergalactic courts'). This directly contradicts the fundamental principle of jurisdiction, which requires a court to have the power to adjudicate cases and issue orders within a defined territory, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'Jurisdiction can be defined as: Power of a court to adjudicate cases and issue orders; or Territory within which a court or government agency may properly exercise its power.' Since 'Martian law' and 'Intergalactic courts' are not recognized legal systems, the agreement becomes unenforceable due to a lack of proper jurisdiction.
